name: Engram Alpha
tagline: Editable graph memory pane inside your IDE
description: Local-first reasoning memory for coding assistants that stores decisions, problems, and insights as a typed graph you can see and edit on a canvas embedded in JetBrains and VS Code.
approachDetail: Typed knowledge graph — 8 node types (Decision, Principle, Caution, Problem, Resolution, Insight, Intent, Anchor) and 7 sentence-shaped edge types (because, replaces, conflicts-with, ...) — in SQLite, with local bge-small embeddings (fastembed/ONNX) in sqlite-vec
searchDetail: Hybrid FTS5 keyword recall + cosine similarity, weighted by computed trust; hits carry up to 5 one-hop neighbors with conflicts/supersessions first; separate bounded graph-traversal tool
storageDetail: One git-ignored .engram/graph.db per repository (WAL, serialized writes); embeddings stored via sqlite-vec
mcpDetail: rmcp stdio server (engram mcp) exposing brief, search, traverse, add_note, link/unlink, update_node/update_edge, approve_node, list_suspects/resolve_suspect, list_open
toolSupportDetail: Ships a Claude Code skill (three capture-aggressiveness variants), a JetBrains plugin (JCEF tool window) and a VS Code extension (secondary-sidebar webview) embedding the same graph pane
scopesDetail: Per-repo graph only; a cross-project layer is explicitly out of scope for v1
duplicateDetail: Write-time same-type cosine pre-check: add_note returns {matched, created: false} at/above the duplicate threshold instead of creating a node; writes near contradicted or superseded knowledge return warnings
retentionDetail: Trust (0..1) is computed at read time from created/last-seen/approved timestamps and fades over ~half a year; a decay pass archives unapproved episodic/volatile nodes 14 days after going stale; replaces-edges auto-archive superseded nodes; hard delete is user-only
multiUserDetail: Single-user, per-repo database; team sharing is explicitly out of scope for v1
crossMachineDetail: The DB is a git-ignored local file; manual JSON export only, no sync
webUiDetail: Vue graph canvas served by the local daemon and embedded in the IDE panes: node editing, conflict-judgment worklist, graph-health strip, provenance, and a Memory Lens showing exactly what the assistant receives
tokenBudgetingDetail: Session-start brief takes a character budget (default ~12k chars, about 3k tokens) and caps sections (e.g. top-8 conflict suspects); search takes a result limit and caps neighbors at 5 per hit
contextInjectionDetail: Cooperative capture: the assistant calls MCP tools guided by a shipped skill (brief at session start, search before non-trivial work, silent batched writes at stopping points); no hooks or automatic injection
networkDetail: Fully local — no remote services or API keys; the bge-small embedding model is downloaded once to a machine-wide cache on first run
auditTrailDetail: Nodes carry provenance (author, session, timestamps) and supersession chains via replaces-edges keep archived predecessors, but there is no full edit-event log
pros: The memory is visible and editable — a graph canvas embedded in JetBrains / VS Code, not a hidden store
Active conflict surfacing: write-time and periodic scans queue look-alike pairs for human judgment (conflict / replaces / dismiss)
Typed, sentence-shaped ontology (a Decision links `because` a Principle) instead of freeform text blobs
Fully local single Rust binary: SQLite in-repo, on-device embeddings, no keys or services
Read-time trust decay: stale memories fade and are eventually archived, computed from timestamps rather than a background job
cons: Single-user, single-machine, per-repo — no sync or team sharing (manual JSON export only)
Cooperative capture depends on the assistant following the shipped skill; nothing is captured ambiently
One daemon per repository to run; onboarding still involves several manual steps
notes: "Alpha" is part of the product name, not a version tag — several unrelated projects are also named "engram". The JetBrains plugin is Kotlin/JCEF; the pane is Vue 3 + Vue Flow.
